question: What is UIKit? How does it relate to SwiftUI in modern iOS apps?
answer:
UIKit is Apple's imperative UI framework for iOS, iPadOS, tvOS and Catalyst — it has powered every iOS app since 2008. Core types include UIView, UIViewController, UIWindow, UINavigationController, UITableView, UICollectionView.

In modern apps:
• Greenfield iOS apps often start with SwiftUI; UIKit is reached for when SwiftUI lacks features (custom transitions, certain gestures, complex collections).
• You can mix the two: UIHostingController hosts SwiftUI inside UIKit; UIViewRepresentable / UIViewControllerRepresentable hosts UIKit inside SwiftUI.
• UIKit still backs all third-party iOS code shipped before iOS 13 and remains the default in many enterprise codebases.

question: Explain the UIViewController lifecycle methods.
answer:
UIViewController progresses through a well-defined lifecycle. Key methods:

• `init(nibName:bundle:)` / `init(coder:)` — VC created (no view yet).
• `loadView()` — view is being instantiated. Override only when not using storyboards/xibs and you want full control.
• `viewDidLoad()` — view loaded into memory. One-time setup (subviews, networking). NOT called per-appearance.
• `viewWillAppear(_:)` — about to be visible. Use for refresh-before-display logic, observers.
• `viewDidAppear(_:)` — visible. Start animations, analytics.
• `viewWillDisappear(_:)` / `viewDidDisappear(_:)` — leaving screen. Pause work, save state.
• `viewWillLayoutSubviews()` / `viewDidLayoutSubviews()` — layout pass; can fire many times.
• `deinit` — VC deallocated. Last chance to remove observers (block-based KVO/Notification handles auto-clean).

question: What is the difference between frame and bounds in UIView?
answer:
Both are CGRects on UIView, but interpreted in different coordinate systems:

• `frame` — the view's position and size in its SUPERVIEW's coordinate system. Where to put me, how big I am.
• `bounds` — the view's own internal coordinate system. Origin is usually (0,0); size matches the view. Changing bounds.origin changes what part of myself is visible (this is how UIScrollView scrolls).

If the view has a non-identity transform (rotation, scale), `frame` is undefined / not meaningful — use `bounds` + `center` + `transform`.

`center` is the midpoint in superview coordinates and is symmetrical with frame (changing center moves the frame).

question: How does UIResponder chain handle touches and events?
answer:
UIResponder is the superclass of UIView, UIViewController, UIWindow, UIApplication. Events (touches, motions, presses, key commands) walk a RESPONDER CHAIN until something handles them.

For touches:
1) Hit-testing — UIWindow walks the view hierarchy with `hitTest(_:with:)` to find the deepest view whose `pointInside(_:with:)` returns true. That view becomes `firstResponder` for the touch.
2) Event delivery — `touchesBegan/Moved/Ended/Cancelled` is called on that view. If it doesn't override (or calls super), the event walks UP the responder chain: view → superview → … → vc → vc parent → window → application.

Gesture recognizers attached to the hit view (or any ancestor) can intercept and consume touches before they reach the event handlers.

question: How do UITableView and UICollectionView work? What is reuse?
answer:
Both are scroll-based containers backed by a data source and a delegate.

• Data source supplies cells (and, for collections, supplementary views) for index paths.
• Delegate notifies about selection, sizing, scroll, etc.
• Cell reuse — instead of creating thousands of cells, the view recycles offscreen cells via `dequeueReusableCell(withIdentifier:for:)`. Cells must implement `prepareForReuse()` to wipe state.
• Modern API: UITableViewDiffableDataSource / UICollectionViewDiffableDataSource — declare snapshots of (Section, Item), the framework computes the diff and animates inserts/deletes/moves automatically.
• Compositional layout (UICollectionViewCompositionalLayout) — describe complex grids/lists as composable sections.

question: What is the difference between Storyboards, XIBs, and programmatic UIs?
answer:
Three ways to build UIKit screens:

• Storyboards — visual editor for whole flows, segues between scenes. Pros: quick prototyping, see layout. Cons: merge conflicts, slow Xcode performance on large boards, hidden state.
• XIBs — single-screen / single-view nibs. Pros: visual but small in scope. Cons: still XML; can rot and break silently.
• Programmatic — UIView/UIViewController constructed in Swift. Pros: code-reviewable, refactor-friendly, dynamic. Cons: more code, no visual preview (except SwiftUI bridge or live previews via `Preview`).

Industry trend: large teams favor programmatic (or SwiftUI) for diff-friendliness; storyboards still appear in tutorials and small apps.
